dailyJudgementSnippet: Anthropic won the day because Claude Design generated the strongest front-page enthusiasm, with commenters calling it impressive and useful for rapid mockups and clearer design communication, and that large positive thread outweighed later worries about Claude pricing and reliability in hardware workflows [E1][E2][E4].
winnerExplanation: Anthropic had by far the most substantial HN attention among tracked providers. The rank-1 Claude Design thread delivered strong practical praise around speeding up mockups, briefs, and UI iteration [E1]. That momentum was partially offset by skepticism about Claude 4.7 tokenizer costs and by blunt complaints that Claude Code/Opus hallucinated in circuit-design use cases [E2][E4], but Anthropic still finished with the best combination of sentiment and coverage.

openai.judgementSnippet: Codex got a small lift from a direct comparison where a commenter said a board-design project failed under Claude/Opus but was completed in Codex instead [E5].

anthropic.judgementSnippet: Claude Design drew heavy praise for rapid mockups and faster client communication, but commenters also pushed on Claude 4.7 cost inflation and said Claude Code/Opus hallucinated badly in circuit-design work [E1][E2][E4].

microsoft_copilot.judgementSnippet: GitHub Copilot was mentioned mainly as a pricing gripe, with a commenter complaining its model-use multiplier rose from 3 to 7.5 and framing that as Microsoft reducing its subsidy [E3].

evidence.summary: The 'Claude Design' launch drew strong positive reaction: commenters called the technology impressive, useful for fast mockups, and valuable for compressing client/design feedback loops, with some saying it could become a major hit if Anthropic keeps expanding it.

In the Claude 4.7 tokenizer-cost thread, commenters questioned whether Anthropic's newer models justify higher costs, talked about diminishing returns, and speculated that rising prices may reflect expensive inference.
A commenter complained that GitHub Copilot's model-use multiplier had jumped from 3 to 7.5 and described the change as Microsoft trying to lose money more slowly.

In the 'SPICE simulation -> verification with Claude Code' thread, top comments said Claude/Opus hallucinated board capabilities, made wild claims, and performed poorly beyond very simple circuit tasks.
A top commenter contrasted failed Claude-generated hardware work with finishing the same project in Codex, giving OpenAI a modest positive comparison signal.
